title: Space Pilot 3000

jokes.text: Space. It seems to go on and on forever... but then you get to the end... and a gorilla starts throwing barrels at you.
jokes.setup: Philosophical musing about the infinite nature of space
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking about the video game Donkey Kong
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that establishes both the sci-fi setting and Fry's character. The philosophical opening tricks viewers into thinking profound sci-fi commentary, then reveals it's about a video game. Perfect economy and structure.

jokes.text: And that's how you play the game. You stink, loser.
jokes.punchline: Insulting the kid he's supposedly teaching
jokes.explanation: Establishes Fry's immaturity and loser status. Functional character work but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Hello! Pizza delivery! For, uh... 'I.C. Wiener'? Oh, crud.
jokes.setup: Pizza delivery to what seems like a normal name
jokes.punchline: Realizes it's a prank call - 'Icy Wiener'
jokes.explanation: Classic prank call setup that serves the plot (gets Fry to the cryogenics lab). The pun is juvenile but fits Fry's world perfectly.

jokes.text: I always thought by this point in my life... I'd be the one making the crank calls.
jokes.punchline: His life aspiration was to be the prankster, not the victim
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Fry's pathetic life situation - his highest ambition is making prank calls. Great character-driven humor.

jokes.text: Here's to another lousy millennium.
jokes.punchline: Pessimistic toast about the entire millennium
jokes.explanation: Sets up the millennium change plot point while showing Fry's pessimism. Becomes ironic when he actually gets a new millennium.

jokes.text: Ten! Neuf! Otto! Sabaa! Eksi! Wu! Chaar! Thathu! Ni! One!
jokes.punchline: Countdown is in multiple languages, creating chaos
jokes.explanation: Absurdist take on multicultural New York. Shows the chaos of the modern world but isn't particularly deep.

jokes.text: My parents. My coworkers. My girlfriend. I'll never see any of them again. Ya-hoo!
jokes.setup: Fry realizing everyone he knew is dead
jokes.punchline: He's excited about it instead of sad
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection that subverts expected emotion. Shows Fry's life was so terrible that losing everything is liberation. Excellent structure and character work.

jokes.text: Welcome! To the world of tomorrow! Why do you always have to say it that way?
jokes.punchline: Colleague is annoyed by the theatrical delivery
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about sci-fi clichés. The phrase becomes iconic for the show. Good workplace comedy dynamic.

jokes.text: Cool. Just like in Star Trek.
jokes.punchline: Fry references 1960s sci-fi
jokes.explanation: Shows Fry's fish-out-of-water status and pop culture knowledge. Simple but effective character work.

jokes.text: Can I ask you a question? As long as it's not about my eye. Uh... is it about my eye? Sort of.
jokes.setup: Leela preemptively restricts questions about her eye
jokes.punchline: Fry immediately wants to ask about her eye
jokes.explanation: Great setup/payoff structure. Leela's defensive nature creates the exact situation she wants to avoid. Realistic awkward interaction.

jokes.text: Cool. An alien. Has your race taken over the earth? No. I just work here.
jokes.setup: Fry assumes aliens have conquered Earth
jokes.punchline: Leela just has a regular job
jokes.explanation: Subverts sci-fi expectations - aliens aren't conquerors, they're just workers. Great deadpan delivery from Leela.

jokes.text: My God. A million years.
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong about how much time has passed
jokes.explanation: Shows Fry's poor math skills and general confusion. Simple character humor.

jokes.text: Well, at least here you'll be treated with dignity. Now, strip naked and get on the probulator.
jokes.punchline: Immediately demands humiliating procedure
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic juxtaposition. The word 'probulator' is absurd and memorable. Great bureaucratic satire.

jokes.text: He's your great-great-great-great-great-great-great-great-great-great-great nephew.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously long string of 'greats'
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation joke about time passage. Gets the job done but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: That's great. What's the little guy's name? Professor Hubert Farnsworth. Ew.
jokes.punchline: Disgusted by the old-fashioned name
jokes.explanation: Simple name-based humor. Fry's reaction is believable but not particularly funny.

jokes.text: Delivery boy? No! Not again. Please, anything else.
jokes.punchline: Same terrible job he had before
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - travels 1000 years to escape his crappy job, gets assigned the same job. Great character-driven humor.

jokes.text: Then you'll be fired. Fine. Out of a cannon into the sun.
jokes.punchline: Literally fired from a cannon into the sun
jokes.explanation: Classic Futurama dark humor. Takes the workplace phrase 'you're fired' to absurd literal extreme. Perfect economy and memorable escalation.

jokes.text: Hold still, damn it! I don't have good depth perception.
jokes.setup: Leela trying to implant career chip
jokes.punchline: One-eyed alien has depth perception issues
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific humor. Leela's physical trait becomes a practical limitation. Logical and funny.

jokes.text: Tourist.
jokes.setup: Fry falls off the transport tube
jokes.punchline: Dismissive one-word reaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery. Shows how mundane extraordinary things have become in the future.

jokes.text: Whoa. A real-live robot. Or is that some kind of cheesy New Year's costume? Bite my shiny metal ass.
jokes.setup: Fry questioning if Bender is real
jokes.punchline: Bender's crude, iconic response
jokes.explanation: Iconic Bender introduction. Perfectly establishes his crude personality and becomes the show's most quotable line. Great character work.

jokes.text: It doesn't look so shiny to me. Shinier than yours, meat bag.
jokes.punchline: Bender's comeback about human skin
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that establishes Bender's superiority complex and casual derogatory terms for humans. 'Meat bag' becomes recurring.

jokes.text: Please select mode of death: 'Quick and painless' or 'slow and horrible.' You have selected 'slow and horrible.'
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets Fry's attempt to make a phone call
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark world-building. Suicide booths are absurdly cheerful about offering death options. Great dystopian satire.

jokes.text: You are now dead. Thank you for using Stop-And-Drop... America's favorite suicide booth since 2008.
jokes.punchline: Corporate branding and customer satisfaction for suicide service
jokes.explanation: Extends the suicide booth gag with corporate cheerfulness. Dark but clever.

jokes.text: Welcome to the world of tomorrow. Shut up, Terry.
jokes.punchline: Leela's irritated dismissal
jokes.explanation: Running gag payoff. Leela's annoyance has reached the breaking point. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: Well, that's your job whether you like it or not... and it's my job to make you do your job... whether I like it or not... Which I do... very much.
jokes.punchline: Admits he enjoys forcing people to do jobs they hate
jokes.explanation: Good workplace satire about power-hungry middle management. The pause before 'very much' sells the punchline.

jokes.text: Why would a robot need to drink? I don't need to drink. I can quit anytime I want.
jokes.punchline: Bender gives classic alcoholic denial response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character establishment. Bender has very human flaws despite being a robot. Classic addiction denial lines.

jokes.text: I'm a bender... I bend girders. You kidding? I was a stud. I could bend a girder to any angle. 30 degrees... 32 degrees... you name it. 31...
jokes.setup: Bender boasting about his bending skills
jokes.punchline: The 'impressive' angles are barely different from each other
jokes.explanation: Great deflation of robot superiority. Bender thinks tiny angle variations are impressive skills. Good character comedy.

jokes.text: Yeah, ever since I was six. Well, okay. But I don't want people thinking we're robosexuals...
jokes.punchline: Bender worried about implications, creates 'robosexuals' portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that becomes part of Futurama lexicon. Addresses robot-human relationships in funny way.

jokes.text: so if anyone asks, you're my de-bugger.
jokes.setup: Need for cover story for their friendship
jokes.punchline: Computer programming term as relationship euphemism
jokes.explanation: Good computer pun that works in context. Shows Bender's awkwardness about friendship despite wanting it.

jokes.text: Welcome to the Head Museum. I'm Leonard Nimoy. Spock? Hey, do the thing. I don't do that anymore.
jokes.punchline: He's tired of doing his famous Spock bit
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about actors being typecast. Nimoy himself being tired of Spock references. Good celebrity cameo usage.

jokes.text: We share our wisdom with those who seek it. It's a life of quiet dignity. Feeding time.
jokes.setup: Heads have dignified existence sharing wisdom
jokes.punchline: Immediately interrupted by feeding like animals
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing. The 'dignity' is immediately undercut by being treated like zoo animals. Good structure.

jokes.text: Yeah, well if you're sorry then why are you doing it? It's my job. You gotta do what you gotta do.
jokes.punchline: Another 'gotta do what you gotta do'
jokes.explanation: Running gag callback. Shows how the phrase is used to justify everything. Building the pattern for bigger payoff.

jokes.text: That's it. You just made my list. Stop it! Down boy! Bad president.
jokes.punchline: Fry treats the former president like a misbehaving dog
jokes.explanation: Absurd situation handled perfectly. Treating Nixon like a pet is both disrespectful and hilarious. Good character dynamic.

jokes.text: I'm going to get 24th century on his ass.
jokes.punchline: Updates 'medieval on your ass' to future century
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective updating of familiar phrase for future setting. Shows how language evolves.

jokes.text: No one makes fun of my nose.
jokes.punchline: She's sensitive about her nose, not her eye
jokes.explanation: Subverts expectation - we think she's sensitive about her eye, but it's actually her nose. Good character detail.

jokes.text: Damn. You guys were totally out of control. That's our job. We're peace officers.
jokes.punchline: Officer justifies violence as their job as 'peace' officers
jokes.explanation: Good irony about police work. The title 'peace officers' contradicts their violent behavior.

jokes.text: Yeah, you know the law... You gotta do what you gotta do.
jokes.punchline: Third use of the catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Running gag builds momentum. Now being used to justify police violence, showing how hollow the phrase is.

jokes.text: We can get out of here... if you just bend the bars. Dream on, skin tube.
jokes.setup: Fry asking Bender to bend bars
jokes.punchline: Bender refuses with another derogatory human term
jokes.explanation: Another creative derogatory term for humans. 'Skin tube' is particularly visceral and weird.

jokes.text: If someone programmed you to jump off a bridge would you do it? I'll have to check my program. Yep.
jokes.setup: Classic parental argument about following others
jokes.punchline: Bender literally would because he's programmed
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of classic parental wisdom. Takes familiar phrase and gives it literal robot logic. Perfect character-specific humor.

jokes.text: That's what separates people and robots... from animals and animal robots.
jokes.punchline: Includes 'animal robots' as separate category
jokes.explanation: Absurd taxonomy that reveals the weird logic of robot/animal distinctions in this world. Good world-building humor.

jokes.text: From now on, I'm going to bend what I want... when I want, who I want. I am unstoppable!
jokes.punchline: Dramatic declaration about bending things
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Bender's rebellious streak. The grandiosity is funny given it's about bending.

jokes.text: You can't possibly understand... what it feels like to be so alone. I understand. I'm the only one-eyed alien on this whole planet.
jokes.setup: Fry claiming no one understands his loneliness
jokes.punchline: Leela is literally the only one of her kind on Earth
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment that bonds Fry and Leela. Shows how self-absorbed grief can be while establishing Leela's deeper loneliness.

jokes.text: What is the matter with you? I just wanted to be part of the moment.
jokes.punchline: His excuse is wanting to participate in the moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender logic - theft is justified as emotional participation. Shows his warped understanding of friendship.

jokes.text: We're unemployed... but we have a doddering old relative to mooch off of.
jokes.punchline: Sees elderly relative as opportunity to freeload
jokes.explanation: Bender immediately sees the angle. 'Doddering' is a great word choice that shows his calculating nature.

jokes.text: I don't have an Uncle Fry. You do now. By God, I am your nephew.
jokes.punchline: Instant recognition and relationship establishment
jokes.explanation: Quick establishment of family connection. The Professor's instant acceptance is endearing if simple.

jokes.text: Can we have some money? Oh, my, no.
jokes.punchline: Professor's polite but firm refusal
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat. Bender's shameless immediately deflated by Professor's gentle but clear boundary.

jokes.text: and here's where I keep assorted lengths of wire. Let me show you... some of the different lengths of wire I used.
jokes.punchline: Obsessed with mundane wire organization
jokes.explanation: Establishes Professor's absent-minded scientist character. His excitement about wire lengths is endearingly nerdy.

jokes.text: Let me show you... some of the different lengths of wire I used.
jokes.punchline: Instead focuses on wire collection
jokes.explanation: Continues Professor's obsession with mundane details over amazing inventions.

jokes.text: We have you partially surrounded.
jokes.punchline: Admits they only 'partially' have them surrounded
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of police drama clichés. The honesty about partial surrounding is absurdly undermining.

jokes.text: Well, we're boned.
jokes.punchline: Bender's crude but matter-of-fact assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender deadpan. 'Boned' becomes his signature way of assessing bad situations. Great economy.

jokes.text: I suppose it is technically possible... though I am already in my pajamas.
jokes.setup: Suggestion to escape in the spaceship
jokes.punchline: Professor's main concern is his pajamas
jokes.explanation: Perfect elderly character priorities. In a crisis, he's worried about proper dress. Good character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Can anyone drive stick? I can... As long as I don't have to parallel park.
jokes.setup: Need someone to pilot the spaceship
jokes.punchline: Leela treats spaceship like a car with parallel parking anxiety
jokes.explanation: Funny juxtaposition of advanced spaceship technology with mundane driving concerns. Relatable anxiety.

jokes.text: I can't see nothin'. Pretty, though.
jokes.punchline: Still appreciates the aesthetic while unable to see
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between practical problem and aesthetic appreciation. Nice character beat.

jokes.text: Oh, those poor sons of... But that's not important. The important thing is, I need a new crew.
jokes.setup: Asked about what happened to previous crew
jokes.punchline: Starts to mention their fate but dismisses it as unimportant
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about workplace safety. Professor's casual dismissal of crew deaths establishes his character priorities.

jokes.text: Are we going to fly through space... fighting monsters and teaching alien women to love?
jokes.punchline: References classic sci-fi clichés, especially Kirk-style alien romance
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about sci-fi tropes. 'Teaching alien women to love' is a perfect Captain Kirk reference.

jokes.text: So... I'm going to be a... delivery boy? Exactly. All right! I'm a delivery boy!
jokes.setup: Realizing he's back to his old job
jokes.punchline: Complete reversal from his earlier horror to enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Perfect character arc completion. Fry's attitude completely reverses because now he has friends and adventure. Great callback structure showing growth.

tmdb_overview: On New Year's Eve 1999, Pizza Delivery boy Philip J. Fry accidentally falls into a cryogenic chamber and is frozen for 1,000 years. Finally unfrozen, he explores New New York, meets his new best friend (a kleptomaniacal robot named Bender) and goes to work for his great-great-great-great-grand-nephew's space delivery business.
